meta.disclaimer: Model simulation outputs for information and entertainment only — not betting odds or guarantees.
meta.model_note: Probabilities come from simulating the whole tournament many times from current team strength; they update as real results come in.

third_place_race.needs: Algeria are mathematically safe as a locked third-place team: at most 5 other third-place records can beat their 4 pts / GD -2 / GF 5, and eight third-place teams qualify.

thresholds.beats_rival_if: ALG overall GD > rival GD, else GF, else fair-play, else FIFA rank (rank 28)

locked_record.statement: Algeria are mathematically safe as a locked third-place team: at most 5 other third-place records can beat their 4 pts / GD -2 / GF 5, and eight third-place teams qualify.

narration.headline: Socceroos Chase History in Dallas Against Unbeaten Egypt
narration.body: Australia stand on the verge of history in Dallas, chasing their first-ever World Cup knockout victory against an Egypt side that has reached this stage for the first time. Our prediction model places the Socceroos as favourites with a 58% chance to win this single-elimination tie, backed by their defensive resilience. Australia ground out two clean sheets in the group stage, while Egypt arrive unbeaten after scoring five goals but failing to keep a single shutout. The sides last met at the World Cup in a scoreless draw, and this clash promises similar tension. Australian coverage highlights a confident camp, with defender Aziz Behich noting that "belief goes through the roof" as they seek to break their knockout drought. Meanwhile, Egyptian fans are riding a wave of pride, though local reports indicate anxiety over captain Mohamed Salah's hamstring strain. Egypt's fiery coach Hossam Hassan remains defiant despite squad injuries, declaring, "I have lions with me, and I'm not afraid of anyone." Watch for Australia's newly configured flanks to test Egypt's reshaped defensive spine in what promises to be a physical, high-stakes battle.

narration.headline: Colombia's High Ambitions Meet Ghana's Resilient Underdog Spirit in Kansas City
narration.body: Colombia enter the Round of 32 riding a wave of buoyant national pride after topping their group unbeaten, while Ghana celebrate reaching the knockout stages for the first time since 2010. Our knockout model heavily favours Colombia with a 92% chance of advancing, reflecting their superior group-stage form and rating advantage. Colombia's head coach Néstor Lorenzo has raised expectations, stating, "Vamos por más y ojalá lleguemos a lo más alto para dejar a Colombia lo más arriba." However, Colombia must sharpen their finishing after a goalless draw with Portugal left players with what midfielder Richard Ríos described as "a bit of bitterness." Ghana, arriving as underdogs with an 8% advancement chance, have built their run on defensive resilience under veteran coach Carlos Queiroz, who insists "the real world championship starts in the next round." Rebuilding through a severe injury crisis that sidelined key stars, the Black Stars have found a new hero in domestic-league goalkeeper Benjamin Asare, who stepped in to record two clean sheets. These sides last met in a scoreless draw during the 2026 group stage, and this single-elimination rematch will be decided by whether Ghana's compact, defensive block can withstand Colombia's fluid, front-foot attacking style.

narration.headline: Holders Meet Fairytale Debutants in Miami David-vs-Goliath Clash
narration.body: The reigning world champions face the ultimate fairytale story in Miami as tournament debutants Cape Verde, the smallest nation by population ever to reach the knockouts, set up a historic single-elimination tie against Argentina. Our knockout model places Argentina as overwhelming favourites with a 96% chance to advance, backed by their perfect group-stage campaign where they scored eight goals and conceded only once. Cape Verde arrive with a 4% chance after a resilient, unbeaten group run of three straight draws, including holding Spain and Saudi Arabia goalless. Argentina's attack is spearheaded by Lionel Messi, who has already scored six goals in this tournament. For Cape Verde, midfielder Deroy Duarte has been the engine holding their shape together. The sides have met once before, fighting to a scoreless draw in their 2026 World Cup group match. Argentina enter under pressure to defend their crown, with national coverage framing this as a stepping stone, though defender Nicolas Otamendi warned, "Cape Verde got the results it got for a reason." Meanwhile, Cape Verde play with absolute freedom, with midfielder Deroy Duarte asking, "So why not?" Watch for Cape Verde's disciplined defensive block trying to stifle Messi's fluid, positionless attack in a classic battle of survival versus supreme quality.

narration.headline: Petković Reunion Adds Emotional Edge to Switzerland and Algeria Knockout Clash
narration.body: This Round of 32 single-elimination tie is defined by a poignant reunion, as Algeria head coach Vladimir Petković faces Switzerland, the national team he managed for seven years. Our knockout model positions the Swiss as favourites with a 69% chance to win the tie, backed by their unbeaten run to top Group B. Algeria, who advanced as a best third-placed team with a 31% chance, must overcome defensive vulnerabilities after a chaotic group stage. Switzerland arrive in buoyant mood following a 2-1 win over Canada, led by breakout 20-year-old forward Johan Manzambi, who has scored three goals this tournament. Algeria counter with captain Riyad Mahrez, who scored twice in their dramatic 3-3 draw with Austria. Swiss coach Murat Yakin remains confident, stating, "I trust my players. We have a seasoned team, and they know how to handle stress very well." Meanwhile, Petković knows his former side's mechanisms, calling them "a solid block" but insisting Algeria will fight to progress. Watch for Switzerland's disciplined midfield trying to stifle Mahrez and Algeria's dangerous transitions in what promises to be a highly tactical battle.

narration.headline: European Champions Face Historic Underdog in Los Angeles Clash
narration.body: A classic style clash takes center stage in the Round of 32 as Spain's possession game meets Austria's intense, physical pressing. Our knockout model positions Spain as heavy favorites with an 86% chance to advance, backed by their flawless group-stage defense where they kept three consecutive clean sheets. Austria, entering their first World Cup knockout match in decades, hold a 14% chance after rescuing their tournament in a chaotic 3-3 draw with Algeria. Spain's attack will rely on Mikel Oyarzabal, who has already struck twice in the tournament, while managing reported injuries to their starting wingers. Spanish coverage suggests the team is expected to win comfortably, though manager Luis de la Fuente warned his squad after their final group match: "It has been a match that has tested us once again, and the team always responds." Austria arrive on an emotional high, but midfielder Marcel Sabitzer was critical of their defensive lapses, calling parts of their last performance "unseriös" and noting there is much to analyze. To pull off a historic upset, Austria must disrupt Spain's rhythm early, while Spain must remain patient to unlock a compact, highly motivated opponent.

narration.headline: Ronaldo and Modrić Meet in Toronto for One Last World Cup Dance
narration.body: This Round of 32 clash in Toronto is defined by a legendary reunion, bringing former Real Madrid teammates Cristiano Ronaldo and Luka Modrić together in what is likely the final World Cup chapter for both icons. Our knockout model positions Portugal as the favorites with a 60% chance to win the tie, reflecting their deep squad quality despite a flat group stage. Croatia hold a 40% chance to advance, with their tournament-hardened core ready to play the role of dangerous underdogs. Portugal arrive unbeaten but searching for cohesion, having followed a dominant win over Uzbekistan with a stagnant goalless draw against Colombia. Croatia rebounded from an opening loss to England by grinding out wins over Panama and Ghana, showing their trademark resilience. History is on Portugal's side, having won four of their nine historical meetings, including a painful extra-time knockout win at Euro 2016. Sourced reports indicate a tense mood in Portugal, where press coverage has called recent displays flat, while Croatia's camp remains defiant. As Croatia captain Luka Modrić noted to CNN Portugal, "Portugal's players are phenomenal, they are a great team," but insisted his side has nothing to fear. Watch for Portugal's star-studded attack to test Croatia's disciplined, veteran-led defensive block in a high-stakes battle of wills.

narration.headline: Golden Generations Collide in Seattle as Belgium and Senegal Meet in the Knockouts
narration.body: This single-elimination tie in Seattle brings together two proud nations whose veteran cores are chasing one final, historic World Cup run. Our knockout model places Belgium as the narrowest of favourites with a 51% chance of advancing, leaving Senegal right on their heels at 49%. Belgium earned their spot by winning Group G, recovering from two opening draws with a resounding 5-1 victory over New Zealand. Senegal, meanwhile, endured a more turbulent path, bouncing back from defeats against France and Norway to thrash Iraq 5-0 and squeeze through as a best third-placed team. The Lions of Teranga carry a potent goal threat in Iliman Sarr, who has already scored three times in the tournament, and Pape Gueye, who enters fresh off a two-goal performance. These sides have met just once before, fighting to a draw in their 2026 group-stage encounter. For Belgium, this tournament represents a final chapter for their celebrated generation, with midfielder Amadou Onana admitting before the draw that he desperately wanted to avoid facing his mother's homeland. Senegal's Pape Bouna Thiaw has kept his squad focused, warning that "even though we scored five goals, there are still improvements to be made." Expect a tactical battle where Belgium's experienced midfield seeks to control the tempo against Senegal's explosive, direct counter-attacks.

narration.headline: Co-Hosts Face Historic Underdogs in High-Stakes Round of 32 Clash
narration.body: This single-elimination tie brings a classic clash of expectations as the United States, riding their best-ever group performance, meet a Bosnia and Herzegovina side playing in their first-ever World Cup knockout round. Our knockout model favors the co-hosts, giving the USA a 75% chance to win and advance, backed by their strong Group D run and superior rating gap. Bosnia and Herzegovina hold a 25% chance to pull off the upset, arriving with a house-money mentality after advancing as a best third-placed team. The Americans enter with momentum after winning two of their three group matches, while Bosnia and Herzegovina clinched their historic spot following a decisive 3-1 win over Qatar. The sides last met in the group stage of this tournament, playing out a scoreless draw. For the USA, manager Mauricio Pochettino has focused on restoring captain Christian Pulisic's fitness, framing this phase as "a final the squad is ready for." Meanwhile, Bosnia and Herzegovina head coach Sergej Barbarez has unburdened his team, stating, "All of this is a bonus for us." Watch for how Bosnia's physically imposing lineup handles the high-pressing style Pochettino has established for the United States.

narration.headline: Heavyweights Meet History Makers in Atlanta
narration.body: A historic first-ever knockout appearance for Congo DR sets up a fascinating clash of expectations against an England side carrying its familiar tournament pressure. Our knockout model positions England as clear favourites with an 86% chance to win, backed by their superior rating gap and an unbeaten group stage. Thomas Tuchel's side arrives with two wins and two clean sheets, though pundits have flagged concerns over their midfield creativity. Congo DR, entering their first World Cup knockout match in history after a 52-year absence, advanced as the best third-placed team following a thrilling 3-1 comeback win over Uzbekistan. The match features a direct battle of in-form goalscorers, with England's Harry Kane and the Leopards' Yoane Wissa both having scored three goals so far. While England topped Group L, the national mood remains a mix of optimism and anxiety, with pundits noting they are still searching for their best XI. For Congo DR, the tournament is already a triumph of unity; captain Chancel Mbemba captured the national pride, stating, "We're still here; the war isn't over yet. But first of all, I want to say thank you, because it's been 52 years." Expect England to dominate possession, while the Leopards look to strike on the counter.

narration.headline: Co-Hosts Mexico Face Resurgent Ecuador in High-Stakes Mexico City Clash
narration.body: A historic, perfect group stage meets a dramatic, tournament-saving comeback as co-hosts Mexico face Ecuador in a single-elimination Round of 32 tie. Our knockout model names Mexico the slight favourite with a 53% chance to win, backed by their flawless Group A run where they took nine points without conceding a single goal. Ecuador, holding a 47% win chance, arrive with massive emotional momentum after rescuing their campaign with a historic 2-1 comeback victory over Germany. Mexico's manager Javier Aguirre has sought to temper the home crowd's high expectations, telling reporters that "it is anecdotal to finish as leader today... in the end what counts is the final placing." Meanwhile, Ecuador's head coach Sebastian Beccacece, who survived intense early pressure, has demanded respect for his squad after their dramatic qualification. The tactical battle will test Mexico's perfect defensive record, marshalled by goalkeeper Tala Rangel, against an Ecuadorian attack revitalized by Nilson Angulo and Gonzalo Plata. History also looms large: this is a rematch of their only previous World Cup meeting in 2002, which Mexico won 2-1, and Ecuador will have to overcome a hostile atmosphere in Mexico City to settle the score.

narration.headline: Norway's Golden Generation Meets History-Making Ivory Coast in Dallas
narration.body: This single-elimination tie in Dallas brings together two nations riding waves of historic momentum. Norway enter as strong favourites with our knockout model giving them a 75% chance to advance, backed by a potent attack that struck eight times during the group stage. Stale Solbakken's side secured two wins before resting key starters in their final group match to prepare for this moment. Ivory Coast, carrying a 25% win chance, arrive having reached the knockout rounds for the first time in their history after securing two group-stage wins and opening the scoring in all three of their matches. The main threat for Norway is talisman Erling Haaland, who has already scored four goals in the tournament. The sides last met in a tight 0-0 draw during the 2026 group stage, but the stakes are now absolute. Ivorian press coverage suggests the squad is highly motivated, with midfielder Christ Inao Oulai stating, "France or Norway, we are preparing to win." Meanwhile, Norwegian captain Martin Odegaard has embraced the pressure, saying, "It is allowed to dream big, and we are going to go for it." Watch for a fascinating tactical battle on the flank between Ivory Coast's direct wingers and a rested Norwegian backline.

narration.headline: Heavyweights Meet Resilient Underdogs in New York Knockout Clash
narration.body: A classic tournament heavyweight meets a resilient underdog as the knockout stage begins in New York. France enter this single-elimination tie as overwhelming favourites, with our prediction model giving them a 90% chance to advance. This projection is backed by a flawless group stage where Les Bleus won all three matches and scored ten goals, powered by the tournament's joint-top scorers Kylian Mbappé and Ousmane Dembélé, who have netted four goals each. Sweden, by contrast, arrive with a 10% chance after squeezing through as a best third-placed team, recovering from a heavy defeat to the Netherlands with a gritty draw against Japan. History shows a competitive edge, with France winning three and Sweden two of their six historical meetings, including a scoreless draw in their last World Cup matchup. For France, supporters expect a deep run, though manager Didier Deschamps has deflected pressure, stating that "France legitimately aspires to win the title" while naming Spain as favourites. Sweden's fans are already proud of meeting their target, but the camp faces anxiety over a reported thigh injury to defender Isak Hien. The key battle will be whether Sweden's makeshift defence can withstand France's frightening, in-form attack.

narration.headline: Brazil and Japan Clash in Houston with Heavy National Stakes
narration.body: A compelling rematch takes center stage in Houston as Brazil face Japan in a single-elimination knockout tie, carrying the memory of Japan's historic comeback victory over the Seleção last autumn. Our knockout model positions Brazil as the favorite with a 63% chance to win, backed by their strong group-stage form where they remained unbeaten and kept two clean sheets. Japan arrive with a 37% win chance after their own undefeated group run, showing immense defensive resilience. Brazil's attack is spearheaded by Vinícius Júnior, who has already scored 4 goals this tournament, alongside Matheus Cunha with 3 goals. Historically, Brazil have won three of their five meetings, but Japan's official records show their recent 3-2 victory in Tokyo was their first ever over the five-time champions. For Brazil, discovery-tier reports describe a "win-or-bust" pressure to end a two-decade title drought, with manager Carlo Ancelotti warning, "Now it's knockout. Need a strong heart." Meanwhile, Japanese coverage indicates a buoyant mood as they aim to finally break their round-of-sixteen barrier, with coach Hajime Moriyasu stating, "I personally am looking forward to being able to face the real Brazil." Watch for how Japan's collective defensive block copes with the central attacking freedom of Vinícius Júnior.

narration.headline: Oranje Attack Meets Unbeaten Atlas Lions in Monterrey
narration.body: This Round of 32 clash in Monterrey carries a deep personal edge, as several Moroccan players face former Eredivisie teammates in what local media have dubbed a friendly-rivalry derby. Our knockout model positions the Netherlands as favourites with a 67% chance to advance, backed by a free-scoring group stage where they netted ten goals. However, Morocco hold a 33% chance of pulling off an upset, arriving with high confidence after navigating a tough group unbeaten under new manager Mohamed Ouahbi. The Dutch attack is spearheaded by Brian Brobbey, who has scored three goals this tournament, while Morocco's primary threat comes from Ismael Saibari, who also has three goals to his name. Historically, these sides last met at a World Cup in a tight group-stage encounter, but single-elimination stakes change everything. Dutch media report that while foreign outlets view the Netherlands as title contenders, local fans remain anxious about a shaky defence. Meanwhile, Morocco's camp is buoyant, with coach Ouahbi stating that "the players now believe in their ability, and opponents respect Morocco." Watch for the battle on the Dutch left flank as they attempt to contain Morocco's star right-back Achraf Hakimi.

narration.headline: Germany Face Physicality Test Against Stubborn Paraguay in Boston
narration.body: This Round of 32 clash presents a classic tournament contrast: a heavy favorite seeking to restore its proud footballing identity against a defensive underdog back in the knockouts after a sixteen-year wait. Our knockout model favors Germany with a 65% chance to win the tie, reflecting their superior rating and ten-goal group-stage haul. However, Julian Nagelsmann's side enters under pressure following a jolting 2-1 final-group defeat to Ecuador that exposed defensive fragility. Paraguay, who advanced with a 35% win chance, arrive with two clean sheets from three matches and a reputation as compact giant-killers. History suggests a tight battle; the sides last met at the World Cup in a scoreless draw. Germany will look to danger man Deniz Undav, who has three goals and two assists, to unlock a stubborn defense. Yet, German coverage describes the squad as disillusioned, with Nagelsmann conceding his team lacks the physical robustness to match their opponents in duels. Meanwhile, Paraguay's camp is fueled by belief, with captain Gustavo Gomez declaring, "When a paraguayan believes, nothing is impossible." Expect a fascinating tactical struggle as Germany's creative passing tries to bypass Paraguay's deep, physical block.

narration.headline: Historic Firsts Collide as Canada and South Africa Meet in Los Angeles
narration.body: A historic milestone anchors this single-elimination tie, as both nations reach the World Cup knockout stage for the very first time. Our knockout model positions Canada as the clear favourite with a 75% chance to advance, backed by their superior group-stage goal-scoring form and a higher rating. South Africa, holding a 25% chance, enter as the ultimate wild card after a historic group-stage run. Canada arrive with two wins and nine goals scored, though a final-day loss to Switzerland cost them home advantage. South Africa bounced back from an opening defeat to secure their spot with a disciplined win over South Korea. Canada's primary threat lies with Jonathan David, who has already scored three goals this tournament. The sides last met in 2026, resulting in a tight 1-0 victory for Canada. South African coverage suggests Bafana Bafana view further progress as a bonus, with coach Hugo Broos calling another win "a miracle." Meanwhile, Canadian reports indicate high expectations, though manager Jesse Marsch admitted disappointment over losing home advantage. Watch for Canada's aggressive transition play to test South Africa's compact, young defensive block.

narration.headline: Eustaquio Strikes Late as Canada Edge South Africa to Reach Last Sixteen
narration.body: Canada have advanced to the Round of 16 after a dramatic 1-0 victory over South Africa at SoFi Stadium. The decisive moment arrived in the 90th minute when Stephen Eustaquio found the back of the net to break Bafana Bafana hearts. The final scoreline reflected the overall balance of play, as Canada's matured expected goals of 1.32 to South Africa's 0.13 confirmed their attacking dominance. Eustaquio was the standout performer on the pitch, earning a match rating of 8.6 while delivering five key passes alongside his match-winning goal. With this victory, Canada's historic first-ever knockout run continues as they prepare to play in the Round of 16 against the winner of the other tie. For Canada, the win brings immense national relief and justifies the optimism surrounding their co-host campaign, especially with captain Alphonso Davies fit to return to action. South Africa exit the tournament with their heads held high; despite the late heartbreak, reaching the knockout stage for the first time in their history has been hailed back home as a watershed moment and an absolute triumph for South African football.

narration.headline: Uzbekistan's Tournament Comes to an End
narration.body: Uzbekistan's journey at the World Cup has reached its conclusion. With three matches played and no points on the board, our group-stage model shows they have a 0% chance of reaching the knockouts. It is a tough, dignified end for a side that fought hard but found the step up in class unforgiving. In Group K, Uzbekistan finished adrift at the bottom of the standings, well off the pace of Portugal, who secured their passage with five points. Colombia topped the group with seven points, while Congo DR finished third on four points. Having lost all three of their matches—including a 5-0 defeat to Portugal and a 3-1 loss to Colombia—Uzbekistan can no longer reach the Round of 32 through either the top-two spots or the third-place wildcard route. The group stage is complete, and while the adventure ends here, the team departs with valuable tournament experience for the future.

narration.headline: Job Done: USA Secure Top Spot and Knockout Place
narration.body: The USA have achieved their primary goal in style, securing their place in the knockouts with absolute certainty. By topping Group D with six points, they have bypassed any complicated math or reliance on the third-place back door, moving forward with complete control over their path. In the final group standings, the Americans finished comfortably ahead of Australia and Paraguay, who both ended on four points, while Türkiye finished at the bottom of the pile. This historic group stage has energized host-nation fans, even as some observers maintain a degree of caution. Reflecting on the achievement, head coach Mauricio Pochettino noted, "The objective was to finish first, and we are first. Now, it's the next stage." With their ticket to the Round of 32 officially punched, the focus shifts entirely to preparing for the knockout phase.

narration.headline: Uruguay's Tournament Ends in Heartbreak
narration.body: Uruguay's journey has come to an end. With the group stage complete, our tournament simulation shows they have a 0% chance to reach the knockouts. They finish third in Group H with two points, just behind Cape Verde and group winners Spain. While they are level on points with Saudi Arabia, Uruguay claim third place on goal difference. However, finishing third is not enough this time. In the cross-group comparison, Uruguay are mathematically out of the best-third places. They sit as the twelfth-best third-placed team, one point behind Senegal at the cutoff. Because eleven other third-place records beat Uruguay's locked record of two points and a minus-one goal difference, the back-door route is completely closed. There are no more matches to watch and no scenarios left to hope for; Uruguay are officially eliminated from the competition. Uruguay are mathematically out of the best-third places: at least 11 other third-place records beat their locked 2 pts / GD -1 / GF 3, and only eight third-place teams qualify.

narration.headline: Türkiye's Tournament Comes to an End
narration.body: Türkiye's journey has reached its conclusion. With all group matches now complete, our prediction system shows a 0% chance for the team to reach the knockouts. They finish at the bottom of Group D with three points, adrift of the USA, Australia, and Paraguay, who have all secured their places in the Round of 32. Despite a spirited 3-2 victory over the USA in their final match, earlier defeats to Australia and Paraguay left Türkiye in fourth place. Because they cannot finish in the top two or reach the third-place pool, there are no remaining paths to advance. It is a dignified exit for a side that fought until the final whistle but ultimately ran out of room in a highly competitive group.

narration.headline: Tunisia's Journey Comes to an End
narration.body: For Tunisia, the tournament has reached its final whistle. There are no remaining paths, no mathematical lifelines, and no back-door routes left to explore. Our prediction system confirms they have a 0% chance to reach the knockouts, meaning they are officially eliminated from the competition. In Group F, they find themselves adrift at the bottom of the standings with zero points after three matches, well off the pace of Sweden, who finished just above them in third. While Japan and the Netherlands comfortably secured their passage to the Round of 32, Tunisia's three defeats leave them with a dignified exit to contemplate. Even a positive result in their final match against the Netherlands could not alter their fourth-place finish. The group stage is now fully complete, and the final standings are settled.

narration.headline: Sweden Secure Knockout Spot After Group Stage Drama
narration.body: Sweden have officially reached the knockouts, delivering a proud moment for a rebuilding squad. Our group-stage model confirms they have a 100% chance of advancing to the Round of 32. They finished third in Group F behind Japan and the Netherlands, who both ended with a perfect advancement record. While Sweden missed out on a top-two finish, their four points and even goal difference left them in a highly secure position. In fact, as a locked third-place team, they are mathematically safe. This back-door route is completely reliable; they are already through as the second-best third-placed team overall, well clear of the cutoff. Swedish coverage notes that reaching this stage meets their pre-tournament target, though the mood is tempered by defensive worries. Reports suggest centre-back Isak Hien is out for the rest of the tournament with a thigh injury, a blow coach Graham Potter called "devastating for him." Nevertheless, Sweden have rebounded from their earlier heavy defeat to the Netherlands and can now look forward to the knockout rounds with quiet confidence. But first of all, I want to say thank you, because it's been 52 years." Our group-stage model reflects this flawless campaign, showing a 100% chance of advancement. They finished comfortably at the top of a group that featured Austria and Algeria, who both ended on four points, well clear of Jordan. Because they won the group outright, Argentina do not need to worry about the third-place back-door route. The mood around the camp is buoyant, yet grounded. Reflecting on the challenges ahead, midfielder Leandro Paredes recalled the 2022 tournament, warning that "Saudi Arabia was a so-called easy match and we ended up suffering." Head coach Lionel Scaloni has also set a cautious tone, resting Lionel Messi from the start in their final group match to manage his minutes. With their perfect group stage complete, Argentina now turn their focus to the knockout rounds as they begin their title defense.

narration.headline: Algeria Secure Knockout Spot After Group J Drama
narration.body: Algeria have reached the knockouts, delivering the proud return to the Round of 32 that fans have awaited for over a decade. Our group-stage model confirms they have a 100% chance of advancing. While they finished third in Group J behind Argentina and Austria, their place in the next round is mathematically secure. They are currently ranked as the sixth-best third-placed team, comfortably inside the qualifying zone. Because their four points and goal difference are already locked in, they are guaranteed to move on. This reliable back-door route serves as a perfect safety net, meaning they are completely safe regardless of other group results. The achievement brings immense relief to a nation still healing from missing out on the 2022 tournament. As captain Riyad Mahrez told Shihab Presse, "We deserved to advance based on the level we showed." With their ticket officially punched, the focus now shifts entirely to the knockout stage. Algeria are mathematically safe as a locked third-place team: at most 5 other third-place records can beat their 4 pts / GD -2 / GF 5, and eight third-place teams qualify.

narration.headline: Black Stars Seal Knockout Spot After Group L Drama
narration.body: Ghana have officially secured their place in the knockouts, ending a sixteen-year wait to reach the Round of 32. Our group-stage model confirms the Black Stars have a 100% chance of advancing. While finishing behind England and Croatia in Group L ruled out a top-two finish, Ghana are mathematically safe as a locked third-place team. With four points and a neutral goal difference, they are currently the third-best third-placed team, comfortably clear of the cut-off. Even though they had to rely on the third-place back door, this route was highly reliable, and they are now guaranteed to progress. Coach Carlos Queiroz has already set an ambitious tone for the next stage, telling reporters, "I have just told my players that the real world championship starts in the next round." The achievement has sparked joyful and emotional celebrations across Ghana, rekindling belief after years of near-misses. Ghana are mathematically safe as a locked third-place team: at most 3 other third-place records can beat their 4 pts / GD 0 / GF 2, and eight third-place teams qualify.

narration.headline: England Secure Top Spot to Reach the Knockouts
narration.body: England have safely reached the knockouts, securing their place in the Round of 32 with a calm and assured group-stage campaign. Our group-stage model shows they have a 100% chance of advancing, having finished comfortably at the top of Group L with seven points. While they were in a tight cluster with Croatia and Ghana, England's results kept them well clear of any third-place anxiety. They progressed entirely through the front door, meaning they do not need to rely on the best-third back-door route. This successful passage comes amid heavy national expectations, with media coverage framing anything short of a deep run as a disappointment. Reflecting on the challenge ahead, manager Thomas Tuchel noted, "The bigger the games get, the bigger we will get." With their spot in the next round officially locked in, England can now focus entirely on their upcoming knockout tie.

narration.headline: Brazil Secure Top Spot and Perfect Path to the Knockouts
narration.body: Brazil have achieved their ultimate group-stage goal, securing their place in the knockouts with absolute certainty. By finishing at the top of Group C with seven points, they have cleared their baseline comfortably and avoided any reliance on a third-place back door. Our group-stage model shows they have a 100% chance of advancing, meaning their path forward is entirely in their own hands. They did not do it completely unchallenged, however; Morocco pushed them all the way, finishing neck-and-neck on points and right on their heels. The job is done, but the mood remains focused. As head coach Carlo Ancelotti told Fato Paulista, "Now it's knockout. Need a strong heart." With their ticket to the Round of 32 officially punched, Brazil can now turn their full attention to the do-or-die matches ahead.

narration.headline: Spain Seal Top Spot with Perfect Group Stage Finish
narration.body: Spain have reached the knockouts in the most assured way possible, securing their place in the Round of 32 with absolute certainty. By finishing at the very top of Group H with seven points and zero goals conceded, they have made their passage completely secure. Our group-stage model shows they have a 100% chance of advancement, having comfortably locked up the top spot ahead of Cape Verde Islands, who finished as runners-up. Because they won the group outright, Spain do not need to worry about any third-place calculations or relying on other results. This flawless group campaign has met high national expectations, though Spanish coverage suggests some caution remains after a few laboured attacking displays. As head coach Luis de la Fuente noted after the final group match, "It has been a match that has tested us once again, and the team always responds." Spain now move forward into the knockout bracket with their status as tournament contenders firmly established.

narration.headline: Canada Clinch Knockout Spot Despite Final-Day Slip
narration.body: Canada are officially through to the knockouts. Our group-stage model shows they have secured a 100% chance of advancing to the Round of 32, meaning their place in the next round is mathematically locked in. They finished the group stage as runners-up behind Switzerland, who ended up on top of Group B with seven points. Canada finished in a tight cluster on four points, level with Bosnia and Herzegovina but ahead on goal difference, while Qatar finished at the bottom. Because they secured the second spot, Canada did not have to rely on the third-place wildcard route. While there is some disappointment after a final-day loss to Switzerland cost them top spot, the national mood is lifting with captain Alphonso Davies fit to return. Reflecting on his manager's decision to hold him back earlier, Davies shared that Jesse Marsch "sat me down and reminded me I wasn't ready yet," a choice that has paid off now that the knockouts are here.
